claim: The microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) cast and rolled at 1050°C were systematically studied by alloying a lighter element beryllium (density 1.848 g/cm3).
evidence_text: In this paper, the microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) cast and rolled at 1050°C were systematically studied by alloying a lighter element beryllium (ρ =1.848 g/cm3).

claim: With increasing Be content, the microstructure changes from a single austenite to a dual-phase steel of austenite and ferrite.
evidence_text: Results show that the microstructure of the experimental steel changes from a single austenite to a dual-phase steel of austenite and ferrite with the increase of Be content.

claim: The density of the 1.5 wt% Be steel is approximately 6.25 g/cm3, which is 19.87% lower than a standard steel (7.82 g/cm3) and 3.8% lower than the base Fe–Mn–Al–C steel (6.50 g/cm3).
evidence_text: The density of the new low-density steel is about approximately 6.25 g/cm3 because of the addition of 1.5 wt% Be, which is 19.87% lower than the commonly used steel grade (ρ = 7.82 g/cm3 ) and 3.8% lower than the Fe–Mn–Al–C steel (ρ = 6.50 g/cm3).

claim: Uniaxial tensile tests were performed at room temperature per ASTM E8/E8M-16A using an Instron 5982 with dynamic extensometer at an initial strain rate of 5×10^{-4} m·s^{-1}, with at least three specimens per condition.
evidence_text: Uniaxial tensile tests (ASTM: E8/E8M-16A) were conducted at room temperature using an Instron 5982 universal tester and dynamic extensometer (Instron 2620–601) to measure the strain at an initial strain rate of $5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } { \bullet } \mathrm { s } ^ { - 1 }$ . … Each experimental data was supported by more than three tensile specimens.

claim: Density was measured by Archimedes drainage using a high-precision balance (readability 0.01 g); the average of three independent measurements was reported.
evidence_text: A high-precision balance was used to measure the weight of each sample (up to 0.01 g) and the final density of steel was calculated using the following formula. … For each sample, the average density was determined by three independently measured density values.

claim: XRD patterns (Fig. 1) of as-cast steels show that 0Be and 0.5Be consist of austenite (γ) + partial precipitates, whereas 1.0Be and 1.5Be consist of austenite + ferrite (δ) + partial precipitates; the (110) ferrite peak at 2θ=44.67° intensified with increasing Be, indicating Be promotes ferrite formation.
evidence_text: Figure 1 shows the X-ray diffraction pattern of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe steels. As shown in the figure, the phase composition in 0Be and 0.5Be steel is austenite (γ) + partial precipitates. In 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel, the phase composition becomes austenite (γ) + ferrite (δ) + partial precipitates. As shown in Fig. 1, with the increase of Be content, the X-ray contrast peak of 1.0Be and 1.5Be samples shows that the diffraction intensity of the ferrite phase (110) increased (2θ=44.67°), which further indicated that the addition of Be promoted the formation of the ferrite phase.

claim: In as-cast 0.5Be steel, XRD detected Be11Fe and Be12Fe compounds with an atomic concentration ratio Fe:Be of 18.7:1; in 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels Be2Fe and Be12Fe peaks also appeared, indicating that higher Be content increases the probability of forming additional Fe–Be compounds.
evidence_text: In the XRD of 0.5Be steel, the peaks of the Be11Fe and Be12Fe compound was observed, in which the atomic concentration ratio of Fe and Be atoms of 0.5Be steel was 18.7:1, whereas no other Be compounds were observed. In the XRD of 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel, the Be2Fe and Be12Fe peaks appear around the Be11Fe peaks, which also indicated that the addition of Be increases the concentration of Be atoms around Fe atoms, thereby increasing the probability of forming other compounds between Fe and Be.

claim: Optical micrographs (Fig. 2) show 0Be is fully austenitic; 0.5Be exhibits black dot-like compounds attached to austenite; Be content above 0.5 wt% leads to ancient Chinese character-shaped Be–Fe phase and abundant ferrite in the austenite matrix, consistent with XRD.
evidence_text: As shown in Fig. 2(a), the microstructure of steel without beryllium is completely austenitic. Fig. 2(b) shows the addition of 0.5Be content. Black point compounds begin to appear and attach to the austenite matrix. … when the content of Be in steel reaches a certain level (>0.5 wt%), a compound similar to the ancient Chinese character shape will appear in the matrix, which is known as Be–Fe phase compound. As shown in Fig. 2(c, d), evident Be–Fe phase compounds were observed … and a large amount of ferrite appears in the austenite matrix. This result is consistent with the XRD results shown in Fig. 1.

claim: TEM of as-cast 0.5Be reveals Be12Fe precipitates of size 5–10 μm (Fig. 3a–c) and diffraction spots confirming the Be12Fe phase (Fig. 3d,e); in the 1.5Be matrix, diffraction spots of AlFe3 were observed.
evidence_text: Figure 3 shows the TEM morphology of as-cast 0.5Be steels, and Fig. 3(a) shows the morphology of Be12Fe, in which the size of precipitates is 5–10 μm. … Fig. 3(d, e) shows diffraction spots under locally mages and different crystal band axes corresponding to the Be12Fe compound. … Additionally, selective diffraction was performed on the 1.5Be matrix, and the diffraction spot of AlFe3 was observed.

claim: In the as-cast condition, matrix hardness increases with increasing Be content; austenite hardness increases with Be, and ferrite hardness in 1.0Be and 1.5Be also increases with Be (Table 2, Fig. 5); the Be–Fe phase hardness in 0.5Be could not be measured.
evidence_text: The microhardness of the as-cast steels was measured … The results are shown in Table 2 and Fig. 5. The hardness of the matrix increases with the gradual increase of beryllium content. As shown in Table 2, the hardness of austenite increases with the increase of Be content. The hardness of ferrite observed in 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels also increase with the increase of Be content. … Given the objective problem of the hardness tester, the metallographic hardness of the Be–Fe phase cannot be tested in the 0.5Be steel; thus, the Be–Fe phase cannot be obtained.

claim: For EBSD, samples were electropolished at −20 °C for 50–65 s at 20 V in an electrolyte of perchloric acid and alcohol (ratio 1:9), and EBSD data were analyzed using TSL OIM v8 software.
evidence_text: Electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD, coupled with Hitachi S3400 SEM) was used to investigate the microstructure in the RD-ND (normal direction) plane after the samples were electropolished at −20 °C for 50–65 s at 20 V using an electrolyte consisting of perchloric acid (HClO4) and alcohol (C2H6O). Additionally, the ratio of alcohol to perchloric acid was 1:9. EBSD data were analysed by using TSL OIM analysis v8 software.

claim: TEM samples were mechanically thinned to 40 μm and then twin-jet electropolished in 10% perchloric acid with 90% ethanol at 26.5 V and −20 °C.
evidence_text: The TEM sample was thinned to 40 μm using waterproof sandpaper, and then electrochemical polishing with a dual jet was performed. Electrochemical polishing was performed in 10% perchloric acid with 90% ethanol solution at 26.5 V and −20 °C.

claim: For OM and SEM, samples were ground with waterproof sandpaper up to #5000, mechanically polished with 0.5 μm diamond suspension, and etched with 8% nital; final surface finish was 0.05 μm.
evidence_text: For OM and SEM sample tests, they were prepared according to standard metallographic surface preparation methods. Then, #400, #800, #1500, #2000, #3000 and #5000 waterproof sandpapers, followed by mechanical polishing using 0.5 μm diamond suspension, were used … The microstructure was observed with an 8% nitrate alcohol solution, with a surface finish of 0.05 μm.

claim: XRD was performed on a D/MAX-2500/PC diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation, scanning 2θ from 20° to 100° at 2°/min in continuous mode.
evidence_text: The phase composition of the alloy was analysed using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) equipped with a Cu Kα X-ray tube (D/MAX-2500/PC, ASTM: D5380–93[2014]). … In the 2θ range of 20° to 100°, the scanning speed was 2° per min, and the XRD patterns were collected via continuous scanning.

claim: In the as-cast condition, the austenite matrix hardness increases with Be content from 453.31 HV (0Be) to 479.74 HV (1.5Be); ferrite hardness, present only in 1.0Be and 1.5Be, also increases from 437.40 to 452.54 HV; the Be-Fe phase hardness was measured as 483.50 HV in 1.0Be and 477.16 HV in 1.5Be, but could not be measured in 0.5Be.
evidence_text: As shown in Table 2, the hardness of austenite increases with the increase of Be content. The hardness of ferrite observed in 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels also increase with the increase of Be content. ... Given the objective problem of the hardness tester, the metallographic hardness of the Be–Fe phase cannot be tested in the 0.5Be steel; thus, the Be–Fe phase cannot be obtained.

claim: The phase transition temperature of the 0Be steel is 1278.8 °C; adding Be decreases the phase transition point by approximately 120 °C, with a significant drop at 0.5 wt% Be, an additional decrease of about 1 °C at 1.0 wt% Be, and a further decrease of 5 °C at 1.5 wt% Be.
evidence_text: The temperature of steel without beryllium phase transformation was 1278.8 °C. After adding Be, the phase transformation points of alloy steel changed remarkably, and its temperature point decreased by approximately 120 °C. ... When the Be content was 0.5 wt%, the phase transformation temperature point evidently decreased. With the increase of Be content to 1.0 wt%, the phase transition temperature slightly decreased by approximately 1 °C. When the Be content increased to 1.5 wt%, the phase transition temperature decreased by 5 °C.

claim: After hot rolling at 1050 °C, XRD reveals that 0Be steel consists of austenite and carbide; 0.5Be steel contains Be11Fe; 1.0Be steel exhibits Be11Fe and Be12Fe; and 1.5Be steel shows peaks of Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe, and Be2C.
evidence_text: The microstructure of 0Be steel is austenite and carbide, whereas Be11Fe appears in the microstructure of 0.5Be steel. With the addition of a Be content, Be Fe and Be Fe compounds appeared in 1.0Be steel. The peaks of Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe and Be2C in the 1.5Be steel appear in the XRD pattern of the steels.

claim: The addition of 0.5 wt% Be results in a total elongation of 40.47%, which is attributed to the beneficial effect of small amounts of Be-Fe phase on plasticity; however, when Be content exceeds 0.5 wt%, ductility decreases.
evidence_text: The elongation of 0.5Be alloy steel is 40.47%. When the content of Be exceeds 0.5%, the ductility of the steel decreases. ... The small amount of the Be–Fe phase is conducive to the plasticity of the alloy.

claim: With increasing Be content, yield strength shows an initial increase, then decrease, and final increase, while tensile strength follows the opposite trend; the addition of Be reduces tensile strength but significantly increases total elongation for 0.5Be and 1.0Be.
evidence_text: With the increase of Be content, the yield strength initially increases, then decreases and finally increases, while the tensile strength presents the opposite trend, initially decreases and finally increases. ... The addition of beryllium reduced the tensile strength of steel, but the total elongation of steel with the addition of 0.5Be and 1.0Be increased significantly.

claim: The fracture surface of 0Be steel exhibits dimples with spherical inclusions, dissociation planes, and river patterns; with increasing Be content (1.0Be, 1.5Be), the dissociation surface and cleavage platforms increase, indicating more brittle fracture features.
evidence_text: Based on the enlarged view of area A1, spherical inclusions are more evident in the dimples in the ductile fibre area. Additionally, the distribution of dissociation planes and river patterns can be observed in the macro fracture area A2. ... the dissociation surface at the fracture of 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels gradually increases, and the cleavage platform appears in Fig. 12(c–d).

claim: EBSD IQ maps (Fig. 13) reveal that with increasing Be content, the fraction of ferrite (red area in phase map) increases; KAM maps show stress concentration at carbides and Be-Fe phase precipitates, predominantly at grain boundaries and phase interfaces, with relatively uniform stress distribution in 1.0Be and 1.5Be.
evidence_text: The KAM figure shows that the stress concentration effect is evident at the precipitation location of the carbide and the Be–Fe phase, and the distribution of these precipitates is primarily concentrated at the grain boundary and phase interface. ... The ferrite content in the matrix gradually increases with the gradual increase of Be content, wherein the red area indicates ferrite, and the green area indicates austenite. ... the stress concentration phenomenon shows a relatively uniform distribution.

claim: TEM selected area diffraction confirms the matrix of all rolled steels is primarily austenite; precipitates in 0Be steel are κ-carbides, while in 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be steels, Be2C compounds are identified, with the size of Be2C increasing with Be content.
evidence_text: Based on the selected aperture diffraction in Fig. 15(a2), (b2) and (d2), the matrix is primarily austenite. The precipitates ... are carbides with different Be contents. ... the precipitates in 0Be steel are κ-carbides. At 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel, Be2C compounds were observed by electron diffraction in selected areas. ... the size of Be2C gradually increases with the increase of Be content.

claim: The density of the hot-rolled strips decreased from 6.54 g/cm³ for 0Be to 6.29 g/cm³ for 1.5Be; yield strength was 989.51 MPa (0Be), 1011.54 MPa (0.5Be), 820.57 MPa (1.0Be), 941.87 MPa (1.5Be); ultimate tensile strength was 1222.27 MPa (0Be), 1130.18 MPa (0.5Be), 1074.33 MPa (1.0Be), 1201.58 MPa (1.5Be); total elongation was 22.91% (0Be), 40.47% (0.5Be), 30.71% (1.0Be), 18.33% (1.5Be) after rolling at 1050 °C.

After hot rolling, the matrix (austenite) microhardness was 415.55 HV (0Be), 470.97 HV (0.5Be), 398.39 HV (1.0Be), and 407.01 HV (1.5Be); the ferrite hardness was 349.81 HV (1.0Be) and 371.50 HV (1.5Be).
X-ray diffraction patterns (Fig. 7) show the presence of Be2C peaks in all Be-containing rolled steels, indicating the preferential formation of carbides between Be and C.
evidence_text: As shown in the X-ray diffraction pattern, the peak value of Be2C exists in each steel containing Be. Therefore, the combination of Be and C into carbides is preferred.

claim: In fully austenitic rolled steels the hardness increases significantly with Be content; in dual-phase (γ+δ) steels the average matrix hardness is lower but 1.5Be steel is 9 HV harder than 1.0Be, contributed by both solid-solution strengthening and fine dispersed Be–Fe precipitates.
evidence_text: In all austenitic steels, the hardness of the material increases significantly with the increase of Be content. ... the average hardness value of the matrix is low, because of the high ferrite content, but the hardness value of 1.5Be steel is 9 HV higher than that of 1.0Be steel ... On the one hand, the contribution of hardness is due to the solid solution strengthening of Be. On the other hand, it is attributed to the precipitation of the Be–Fe phase.

claim: In 1.0Be and 1.5Be dual-phase steels, cracks initiate and rapidly propagate inside brittle ferrite grains and are arrested when they reach the adjacent austenite region; final failure occurs after large plastic deformation of austenite.
evidence_text: during 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel (dual-phase steels) quasi-dissociation, cracks initially occur and rapidly expand inside the grains of the brittle ferrite phase, and they are immediately hindered when they expand to the adjacent austenite region with better plasticity. ... Austenite is destroyed after large plastic deformation.

claim: Optical micrographs (Fig. 8) show annealing twins and Be–Fe phase in all rolled strips; 1.0Be and 1.5Be exhibit ferrite (α-Fe) regions alongside γ-Fe.
evidence_text: Fig. 8. OM morphology ... (c) 1.0Be and (d) 1.5Be. ... Annealing twin ... α-Fe ... γ-Fe ... Be-Fe phase

claim: Fig. 13 presents the initial EBSD maps (IQ, IPF, KAM, Phase) of low-density steels rolled at 1050 °C for compositions 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, and 1.5Be.
evidence_text: Fig. 13. Initial EBSD map of low-density steel which rolled at 1050 ◦C. (a1), (b1), (c1) and (d1) shows the 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be of the IQ map; (a2), (b2), (c2) and (d ) shows the 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be of the IPF map; (a ), (b ), (c ) and ... shows the 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be of the KAM map; (a ), (b ), (c ) and (d4) shows the 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be of the Phase map.

claim: In as-cast 0.5Be steel, selected-area diffraction patterns from precipitates viewed along the [−113] and [001] zone axes confirm the presence of Be12Fe.
evidence_text: The diffraction pattern of Be12Fe appears in the SADP taken from the precipitate along the axis of the [− 113] and [001] regions.

claim: In as-cast 1.5Be steel, large Be12Fe precipitates intersect horizontally and vertically as observed by TEM (Fig. 4).
evidence_text: the TEM micrograph of the 1.5Be steel is shown in Fig. 4, and large Be12Fe precipitates intersect horizontally and vertically.

claim: In fully austenitic 0Be and 0.5Be rolled steels, grain size increases with Be addition; with further Be (1.0Be, 1.5Be), the appearance of ferrite leads to a decrease in overall grain size because they become dual-phase steels, as shown by EBSD statistics (Fig. 14).
evidence_text: In the full austenitic structure, that is, the steel structure of 0Be and 0.5Be, the grain size increases after the addition of Be. With the further increase of Be content, the appearance of ferrite in 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel leads to a decrease in overall grain size because they are dual-phase steels.

claim: When excess beryllium cannot dissolve in austenite, it precipitates as spot-like compounds on ferrite, as shown in Fig. 17(b–d).
evidence_text: as shown in Fig. 17(b–d), when the remaining beryllium cannot be dissolved in austenite, the excess beryllium will precipitate on ferrite in the form of spot-like compounds.

claim: In Fe-based alloys, Mn (124 pm), Al (143 pm), and Be (111 pm) form substitutional solid solutions with Fe (124 pm), while C (77 pm) forms an interstitial solid solution (Table 5).
evidence_text: Table 5 shows the atomic size of each element involved in this experiment and the type of solid solution formed. Table 5 Atomic dimensions of elements in steels and the type of solid solution in iron-based alloys.

claim: Larger atomic radius differences increase lattice distortion energy, destabilizing the lattice; exceeding the solubility limit leads to precipitation of Be–Fe phases or compounds.
evidence_text: When the atomic radius difference between each element is greater, the lattice distortion energy will be higher and the lattice will be more unstable. … if we continue to increase the content of Be, a new phase will be formed around the solid solution. The new phase is known as the Be–Fe phase or Be compound.

claim: In a comparison of high-strength metals, 0.5Be steel achieves density of 6.4 g/cm³ and product of strength and ductility of 45.5 GPa%; 1.0Be: 6.4 g/cm³, 32.8 GPa%; 1.5Be: 6.3 g/cm³, 22.0 GPa% (Fig. 20).
Experimentally measured densities are 6.47 g/cm³ for 0.5Be, 6.36 g/cm³ for 1.0Be, and 6.29 g/cm³ for 1.5Be; a gap from theory exists due to preparation loss, phase changes, and lattice expansion.
evidence_text: (1) The density of 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel was 6.47, 6.36 and 6.29 g/cm3 , respectively. A certain gap was observed between the theoretically calculated density and the experimental density of the material. The preparation loss, phase change, lattice expansion and other factors affected the density of the material…

claim: Fig. 15 presents bright-field TEM images of rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe steels, showing microstructures, selected austenite diffraction spots, carbide morphologies, and carbide diffraction spots for each Be content.
evidence_text: Fig. 15. Bright-field TEM images of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe steel after rolling: (a ), (b ), (c ) and ( d1 ) show the structures of 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel, respectively; (a2), (b2) and (d2) show the selected austenite diffraction spots of 0Be, 0.5Be and 1.5Be steels, respectively; (a3), (b3), (c3) and (d3) show the microstructure of carbide in 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be and 1.5Be steel, respectively; and (a4), (b4), (c4) and (d4) show the selected carbide diffraction spots…
